title: Names and labels
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of adding labels. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed:
The attribute goes inside the opening tag:

. The closing tag stays as it is:

.
why.no-class: There is still no label on the two bikes. They are of one kind — if you want to style them the same tomorrow, you must call them the same today.
hint2.no-class: Two of the three paragraphs are the same kind of thing. Which two?
hint3.no-class:
…

— the attribute lives inside the opening tag, before the closing bracket.
hint4.no-class: Put class="bike" on both bikes. The delivery note is NOT a bike — it stays unlabelled.
why.empty-class: There is a class="" — empty. An empty label names nothing. It is like sticking on a blank sticker.
why.id-instead: You used an id on a paragraph. But an id is a PERSONAL name — only one thing on the page may carry it. The two bikes are not one thing, they are two things of one kind. That is what class is for.
hint2.id-instead: Give the same id to two things and nobody knows which one you mean.
hint3.id-instead: And the sly part: the browser will not complain. Repeat an id and it simply grabs the first one and moves on. Nothing crashes — half your code just quietly does not work.
hint4.id-instead: Swap the id for a class on the paragraphs. Save id for something that is one of a kind — the heading at the top, for instance.
why.named-by-look: You named the label by LOOK — "red", "blue", "big", "bold". Tomorrow the boss wants the price green. So either you rename everything, or you leave class="red" on something green — and everyone reading the code will wonder if it is a bug.
hint2.named-by-look: Ask not "how does it look", but "what IS it".
hint3.named-by-look: class="red-text" looks sensible today and is a trap tomorrow. class="price" has no such problem — a price stays a price, whatever you do to it.
hint4.named-by-look: The two paragraphs are not "blue" and "red". They are both bikes. That is the name.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. The task is to add labels, not to delete things. All three paragraphs stay — two of them simply get a name.
blocks.0.text: Three paragraphs. The first two are bikes for sale. The third is a note about delivery. To the browser all three are the same thing: a paragraph.
blocks.1.text: Tomorrow you will want the bikes to look one way and the note another. But how will you tell it which is which?
blocks.4.text: class is a label. You put it on everything of one kind. Here: "these are bikes".
blocks.5.text: One thing can carry several labels at once, separated by spaces: class="bike sold". And one label can sit on a hundred things.
blocks.8.text: id is different. It is a PERSONAL name — only one thing on the entire page may carry it.
blocks.9.text: Like an ID card number: two people cannot have the same one.
blocks.11.items.0: class — a kind. Can repeat. "All the bikes."
blocks.11.items.1: id — a name. Only once. "That one bike."
blocks.12.text: If you are unsure — use class. It covers ninety percent of cases.
blocks.14.text: Neither does anything on its own. Run the page — nothing changes. No colour, no spacing.
blocks.15.text: They are handles. CSS grabs them to style. JavaScript grabs them to change things. And you add them today so you can do that tomorrow.
blocks.16.text: One thing id does on its own
blocks.17.text: There is one case where id earns its keep immediately, with no CSS and no JavaScript.
blocks.18.text: A link to #top takes you to the thing with that id — on the same page, with no reload. That is where tables of contents, "back to top" buttons, and long articles with sections come from.
blocks.19.text: And that is why id must be unique: if two things are called "top", the browser does not know where to take you.
blocks.21.text: Here is the mistake you will keep making for years: name by MEANING, not by appearance.
blocks.22.text: class="red-text" looks sensible today. Tomorrow the boss wants the price green. So either you rename everything, or you call it "red" and make it green — and everyone reading the code will wonder if it is a bug.
blocks.23.text: class="price" has no such problem. A price stays a price, whatever you do to it.
blocks.24.text: Your turn. Give the two bikes a shared label — and name it by meaning.
